segments.title: The Warrior moment works through production as well as design
segments.summary: The opening defines HMS Warrior's impact as a signal of British industrial capacity, not just a single technological leap.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke frames the question around what made Warrior politically and strategically powerful. The ship mattered because Britain paired a striking new armored frigate with the ability to build sisters and follow-ons quickly. That turns the watch point into a lesson about naval deterrence: the visible ship is only convincing when rivals can also see the production system behind it.

evidence.note: Introduces the counterfactual of Warrior as a two-decker and the Royal Navy's existing two-decker option.
Defines the Warrior moment as technological superiority backed by the ability to build many ships.
Contrasts Warrior's industrial signal with Dreadnought's more open challenge to rivals.

segments.title: La Gloire shows why image could outweigh priority
segments.summary: The episode explains why the first ocean-going ironclad is often overshadowed by Warrior despite La Gloire's historical priority.
segments.body: This section separates chronological firsts from lasting naval reputation. La Gloire arrives first and brings important French armored-ship ideas to sea, but her speed, size, machinery, and image limit the shock she creates. Dr. Clarke uses that contrast to show how warship reputation depends on perceived status, visible scale, and whether a design looks like the future others expect.

evidence.note: Introduces La Gloire as the French ocean-going ironclad and lists speed, size, machinery, armor, and gun details.
Explains that La Gloire launched and completed first but is often eclipsed in memory by Warrior.
Argues that La Gloire lacked status even among large frigates such as HMS Mersey and Orlando.

segments.title: Large frigates explain why Warrior was the natural British answer
segments.summary: The historical section places Warrior in a Royal Navy system that used powerful frigates for presence, commerce raiding, and global status.
segments.body: Warrior was not an isolated flash of invention; she grew out of an Admiralty preference for very large frigates. Dr. Clarke describes a fleet split between battle-line strength and global frigate presence, then shows why adding armor to a Mersey-style idea made strategic sense. The useful takeaway is that Warrior won the narrative because she combined recognizable frigate prestige, large size, speed, firepower, and industrial follow-through.

evidence.note: Describes British large frigates, their worldwide presence role, and the Admiralty response to La Gloire.
Compares Warrior's displacement, sisters, trial speed, sailing performance, and status advantages.
Explains Warrior's familiar visual language, mixed armament, armor comparison, and narrative dominance.

segments.title: Ironclad frigates start taking over the battle fleet's job
segments.summary: The video traces how armored frigates and battery ironclads became the battle units around which later ship categories evolved.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke uses the Duncan and Bulwark-era two-deckers to show that the Royal Navy still had line-of-battle options available. Warrior's success pushed attention toward fast armored frigates, built for running fights and individual engagements rather than the old line. That shift helps explain why later terms such as cruiser, destroyer, and battleship emerged from a period when the ironclad frigate had absorbed so much of the future battle role.

evidence.note: Lists contemporary two-decker construction and explains why ironclad frigates became the capability everyone copied.
Argues that ironclad frigates and battery ironclads became the battleships of their era.
Connects the disappearance of frigate phrasing to later cruiser, destroyer, and battleship categories.

segments.title: A stronger French ironclad forces Britain to build upward
segments.summary: The counterfactual begins with a better La Gloire, making a bigger two-decker ironclad the most convincing British way to regain overmatch.
segments.body: The scenario does not assume the historical Warrior was weak; it asks what Britain would do if the French ship looked much harder to overawe. Dr. Clarke points to a conservative Admiralty, Baldwin Walker's comfort with both large frigates and two-deckers, and the political need for a clear visible step above a French armored frigate. In that setting, a slightly better frigate is not enough: a two-decker ironclad becomes the statement ship.

evidence.note: Sets the alternate-history premise: a better La Gloire would require more than the historical large-frigate answer.
Identifies Baldwin Walker, Alexander Milne, and William Martin as the decision-makers, with Walker as the key design influence.
Argues that Britain would build both frigates and a two-decker because only a battleship-sized answer clearly tops a stronger French frigate.

segments.title: The two-decker Warrior becomes a massive gunnery and machinery problem
segments.summary: The design sketch estimates a 91- to 101-gun ironclad with mixed 110-pounders, 68-pounders, heavy armor, and much larger machinery demands.
segments.body: This watch point is the most concrete ship-design section of the episode. Dr. Clarke sketches a two-decker with roughly second-rate gun numbers, 110-pounder rifles, many 68-pounder smoothbores, a Warrior-like armored fighting citadel, and a far more demanding machinery layout. The likely result is a Duncan-derived ship of perhaps 12,000 to 13,000 tons, showing how quickly armor, guns, boilers, and speed turn the idea into a much larger naval-architecture challenge.

evidence.note: Estimates the gun count and likely mix of 110-pounders, 40-pounders, and 68-pounders across two decks.
Discusses the armored citadel, possible machinery arrangements, boiler weight, and speed requirements.
Connects proven Warrior technologies to a Duncan-style two-decker of roughly 12,000 to 13,000 tons.

segments.title: Britain gets separate ironclad battle and frigate fleets
segments.summary: The scenario gives the Royal Navy a dedicated ironclad battle fleet alongside ironclad frigates for global cruising and presence.
segments.body: The force-structure consequence is the key payoff: Britain no longer asks one family of ironclad frigates to cover both global cruiser work and battle-fleet overmatch. Dr. Clarke argues the Royal Navy could justify multiple huge two-deckers while still building ironclad frigates, because the political fear of losing command of the sea would make refusal difficult. This creates a more expensive but more explicit ironclad navy, with different ships for battle and worldwide presence.

evidence.note: Separates the two-decker battle role from the ironclad frigate's chasing and worldwide presence role.
Argues that Britain could justify and build several two-decker ironclads plus matching ironclad frigates.
Describes the resulting split between a battle fleet and a frigate fleet, plus the public signal of an ironclad navy.

segments.title: Two-decker ironclads raise the bar for turrets and Dreadnought
segments.summary: The ending asks how bigger early ironclads would change turret adoption, speed races, and the later need for Dreadnought to overwhelm rather than merely overmatch rivals.
segments.body: The final section follows the second-order effects into later battleship design. If the benchmark is a huge two-decker ironclad with many guns, turret ships may need more turrets, different layouts, or earlier experiments with superfiring to look convincing. Dr. Clarke then connects that changed standard to Fisher's Dreadnought problem: recreating a Warrior moment in this timeline would require an even more dramatic jump in firepower, speed, and perceived superiority.

evidence.note: Explains why later turret ships would need enough visible firepower to compete with a two-decker ironclad benchmark.
Links a changed Warrior moment to Jackie Fisher, possible turret layouts, speed pressure, and altered ship categories.
Argues that Dreadnought would need to overwhelm, not just overmatch, if the earlier Warrior moment had been defined by a much larger jump.
